title: QUIC Connection Runtime Path State and Migration Architecture
purpose: Describe and decompose the runtime-owned active-path and candidate-path model, address-change classification, validation workflow, anti-amplification accounting, and migration promotion rules without expanding into a generalized multipath scheduler.
design_summary:
The connection runtime owns one active path and a bounded set of candidate and recently validated paths. Packet ingress from a new peer address is classified explicitly, then either ignored, treated as a migration candidate, or matched against the recently validated cache. Validation state, amplification state, and recovery snapshots stay per-path so anti-amplification accounting and migration promotion remain deterministic.

Handshake confirmation gates promotion of migration-sensitive behavior. Validation success can mark a candidate validated, update the last validated remote address, and promote the candidate to active path only when the gate allows it. Trusted reuse can skip a fresh probe when the address is still recent. Promotion emits an explicit effect so the endpoint can update binding state and reset path-local transport state consistently.

This architecture is also the decomposition front for the remaining `9000-11-migration-core` umbrella. The current trace-safe execution order is to finish the CRT path-state prerequisites first, reuse the existing RFC 9000 leaf owners for token emission, new-local-address probing and recovery reset, path-validation response and retry, and ECN ACK-count validation, and split a new RFC 9000 artifact family only when a concrete cell cannot be carried by those owners.
alternatives_considered: A generalized multipath scheduler was rejected because the spec only requires one active path plus candidate paths for this slice.
Inlining endpoint rebinding inside the reducer was rejected because binding updates must remain explicit effects.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Unvalidated paths must enforce their own amplification budget before any send effect is emitted.
A new address seen before handshake confirmation may create a candidate path and send validation traffic, but it must not become active prematurely.
Validated candidates should be promoted only after successful validation or a recent-validation reuse rule the runtime treats as trusted.
Promotion must reset or restore path-local transport state using the migration semantics already encoded in the runtime effects.
The recently validated cache must remain bounded so stale addresses cannot bypass the probe and promotion rules.
Recently validated cache eviction and close-versus-reset policy must stay explicit follow-ons if they cannot be proven under this owner.
data_and_state_considerations: Path state is intentionally split into active-path, candidate-path, and recently validated path records so each record can carry its own validation metadata, amplification accounting, and recovery snapshot. The runtime also tracks the last validated remote address as a first-class field to support trusted reuse decisions and explicit change classification.
risks: Recent-validation reuse must remain bounded so stale addresses do not bypass the probe and promotion rules.
Promotion must keep the active-path reset path deterministic or the congestion and recovery state could drift across path changes.
The runtime should remain single-owner for connection state; endpoint binding changes stay effect-driven rather than inline.

supplemental_sections.heading: Migration-Core Decomposition
supplemental_sections.content: `9000-11-migration-core` should execute through this CRT path-state owner before adding new RFC 9000 artifact IDs. The existing supporting RFC leaves are `ARC/WI/VER-QUIC-RFC9000-0002` for server `NEW_TOKEN` emission, `ARC/WI/VER-QUIC-RFC9000-0005` for new-local-address probing and recovery reset, `ARC/WI/VER-QUIC-RFC9000-0010` for `PATH_CHALLENGE` retry and `PATH_RESPONSE`, and `ARC/WI/VER-QUIC-RFC9000-0011` for sender-side ECN ACK-count validation. Future decomposition should name a single concrete missing cell before minting a new RFC 9000 artifact family.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This slice covers path ownership, address-change classification, validation orchestration, amplification accounting, and migration promotion. It does not expand the system into a generalized multipath engine.
